lead.headline: Anthropic plants an always-on AI inside Slack that learns how your company actually thinks
lead.summary: Anthropic launched Claude Tag, a persistent AI presence that lives in Slack, reads conversations it is invited to, and builds an evolving picture of how a company communicates, decides, and uses jargon. Unlike point-in-time chatbots, it can be summoned mid-thread to answer questions using context absorbed over weeks. Anthropic calls it a coworker, not a tool.
lead.takeaway: Anthropic is not just selling AI access — it is competing to own the institutional-knowledge layer before any rival does.
lead.why: This is the enterprise AI play that matters most right now: whoever holds organisational context holds the relationship. For someone pivoting into founder's-office or AI-strategy roles, this model — where AI earns its keep by knowing your company, not just the internet — is immediately relevant. Every major enterprise software vendor will respond with a competing version within months; understanding the architecture now is table stakes.

frontpage.headline: US Congress passes war powers resolution against Trump's Iran conflict — a historic first
frontpage.summary: Both chambers of Congress voted to pass a resolution directing President Trump to halt US military action against Iran, the first time such a measure has cleared both houses in history. The move is constitutionally symbolic — the President can veto it — but signals a growing legislative majority uncomfortable with the conflict. The Senate vote followed the House earlier in the week.
frontpage.takeaway: Congress has drawn a line; the Iran conflict now carries a domestic political ceiling even if it continues operationally.
frontpage.why: The US-Iran war affects India's oil import costs, regional shipping, and diplomatic positioning. A legislative rebuke does not end the conflict but raises the domestic political cost of escalation and opens space for a negotiated off-ramp. India, which has long managed careful neutrality, watches any diplomatic shift closely.

frontpage.headline: A Bengaluru startup is not racing Nvidia on speed — just on price
frontpage.summary: The Ken profiles a Bengaluru AI chip company building inference-optimised silicon designed to undercut Nvidia on cost rather than compete on raw performance. The thesis: most production AI workloads run on inference, not training, and they do not need the fastest chip — just a cheap enough one. The startup is pitching Indian and global cloud players.
frontpage.takeaway: India's best shot at the chip market may be the inference layer, where price beats petaflops.
frontpage.why: For anyone tracking Indian deep tech, this is the clearest articulation yet of a viable wedge strategy. If the startup gets traction, it validates a model where Indian hardware companies compete not on frontier benchmarks but on cost-per-token at scale. Worth watching who funds the next round and whether the IP is defensible.

stories.headline: Trump asked 'what about India?' when JD Vance floated Indian peacekeepers for Ukraine
stories.summary: When Vice President JD Vance raised the possibility of deploying Indian peacekeeping troops as part of a Ukraine ceasefire arrangement, President Trump interjected to question India's role. NDTV reports the exchange in the context of ongoing US-Ukraine diplomacy, reflecting active discussions about neutral third-party monitors for any eventual peace deal.
stories.takeaway: India's name is now in the room when Ukraine peace is discussed — useful leverage, but also a pressure point.
stories.why: India's strategic neutrality on Ukraine has been an asset. Being formally drafted into a peacekeeper role would change that calculus significantly and risk complicating relations with Russia. Track whether this develops into a formal ask through diplomatic channels.

opportunities.name: Qualcomm India Snapdragon Multiverse Hackathon
opportunities.summary: Qualcomm India is running a hackathon to build multi-device AI solutions using Snapdragon hardware. Designed for developers and founders interested in on-device intelligence, edge AI, and hardware-software co-design. Qualcomm hackathons typically include mentorship, hardware access, and paths to visibility with the company's ecosystem.

opportunities.name: PW AI Startup Challenge 2026 (India)
opportunities.summary: Physics Wallah is running a startup challenge for teams building in the AI or education space in India. Worth applying if your idea has an ed-tech or AI angle targeting the Indian market — PW's distribution and brand give winners meaningful exposure.

opportunities.name: IndiaAI Startups Global — International Acceleration Program, 2nd Cohort
opportunities.summary: IndiaAI's global acceleration program connects Indian AI startups with international markets, mentors, and investors. The 2nd cohort is active. Government-backed with real international reach — if you are building an AI product and want global exposure with institutional support, this is one of the more credible pathways available right now.

opportunities.name: SAP Labs India 2026 Startup Studio Cohort
opportunities.summary: SAP Labs India's Startup Studio is taking applications for its 2026 cohort, focused on enterprise AI and deep-tech innovation. Selected startups get access to SAP's enterprise customer network, technology stack, and senior mentors. Strong fit for B2B AI founders who want to sell into large enterprises and need credibility to open those doors.
